straight_beat.name: Hal Cifuentes
straight_beat.column:
The Angels’ latest home loss to Cincinnati felt like a familiar ache, the kind that lingers after a bruising workout. You know the drill: a pitcher throws six innings, allows three runs, and then the bullpen coughs up two in the eighth. Here’s the box score talking.  

The Reds didn’t just beat the Angels—they handed them a lesson in patience. Cincinnati’s offense, which had been sputtering all spring, found its rhythm in the first inning. A two-run homer by someone you’ve probably forgotten by now, and suddenly the Reds were in control. The Angels’ bats, meanwhile, looked like a group of guys trying to hit a moving target. Three runs in the game? That’s the kind of output that makes you wonder if the lineup card was written in a language you don’t speak.  

The starting pitcher, let’s call him “the guy who’s been here since the Jackie Autry era,” lasted six innings. He didn’t give up much, but he didn’t get much either. The Angels’ offense, which had been teetering on the edge of a breakthrough, couldn’t find the gaps. You could see the frustration in the dugout

optimist.name: Sunny Escalante
optimist.column:
The Angels lost yesterday, but let’s not let that overshadow the flicker of hope that kept popping up like a rally monkey in the outfield. Sure, it was a 7-3 defeat, but here’s the thing: the way Rengifo flipped that double play in the third? That’s October magic hiding in April. You know the kind—when the gloves find their spots, the throws are sharp, and the team looks like it’s got a map to the playoffs tucked in its cleats.  

Now, I’m not here to sugarcoat the fact that the Angels are 7-8 and a half game behind in the AL West. But let’s not forget, this isn’t a race where you start at the back of the pack and finish in the dust. It’s a track with turns, and sometimes you’ve got to take a few laps to find your rhythm. The bullpen, for instance, held steady in the late innings, and

pessimist.name: Murray Doblinski
pessimist.column:
The Angels lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your television out the window. Three runs. Three. And somewhere, Arte is laughing into a glass of Cabernet, counting the money Shohei isn’t making him. The same old Halos.  

The offense, that cursed, floundering offense, continues to flounder. They’ve got the tools—Ohtani, the stud pitcher who’s also a slugger, and a bullpen that’s supposed to be “built for the future.” But instead of future, they’re getting a present that’s leaking. Yesterday’s game was a microcosm: a 7-3 drubbing that felt like a 2017 matchup between the Angels and the Astros, except this time, the Astros were the ones laughing.
